title: Errors & try/except
subtitle: Errors aren't scary — they're little notes that help you fix things.
goal: Read a traceback, recognize common Python errors, and use try/except so your program keeps going instead of crashing.

blocks.heading: Errors are friends 🛟
blocks.html:
Everybody's program breaks sometimes — even the pros! When Python hits something it can't do, it stops and shows a message called an error. That message is not Python yelling at you. It's Python saying: “I got stuck right here, and this is why.”

Today you'll learn to read those messages, spot the common ones, and write code that stays calm when something goes wrong. 🎉

blocks.heading: Reading a traceback
blocks.html:
When an error happens, Python prints a traceback. Read it from the bottom up: the very last line tells you the type of error and a short hint. The lines above show where it happened.

Run the box below — it will crash on purpose so you can see what a traceback looks like.

blocks.caption: A crash on purpose (look at the last line!)
blocks.explain: The last line says ZeroDivisionError: division by zero. That's Python telling you that you tried to divide by 0, which is impossible. The line above shows it happened on 'result = number / 0'.

blocks.heading: The common errors (meet the gang) 👋
blocks.html:
• SyntaxError — you typed something Python can't read, like a missing quote or colon.
  • NameError — you used a name Python has never seen (maybe a typo).
  • TypeError — you mixed types that don't go together, like adding a number to text.
  • ValueError — the type is right but the value is wrong, like turning the word "cat" into a number.
  • ZeroDivisionError — you tried to divide by zero.
  • IndexError — you asked for a list spot that doesn't exist.
  • KeyError — you asked a dictionary for a key it doesn't have.
Read bottom to top! The last line of a traceback is the most important — it names the error and gives a one-line hint.

blocks.heading: try / except: catch the error 🪤
blocks.html: Instead of letting the program crash, you can try some risky code and except (handle) the error if it shows up. Python runs the try block; if an error pops out, it jumps to the except block instead of crashing.

blocks.caption: Safe division — no crash!
blocks.explain: When b is 0, the try block raises ZeroDivisionError. Python skips to the except block and returns a friendly message instead of crashing the whole program.

blocks.caption: Catching a specific error: safe int conversion
blocks.explain: int("cat") raises a ValueError because 'cat' isn't a number. We catch ONLY ValueError, so other kinds of problems would still be noticed. Catching specific errors is the polite, careful way.

blocks.html: Be specific! Try to catch the exact error you expect (like except KeyError). A bare except: catches everything and can hide real bugs.

blocks.heading: else and finally ✅
blocks.html:
You can add two optional helpers:

  • else runs only if the try block had no error.
  • finally runs no matter what — error or not. It's great for cleanup or a goodbye message.

blocks.caption: Looking up a missing dictionary key
blocks.explain: For 'Mia' the key exists, so the else block runs. For 'Alex' there's no key, so KeyError fires and the except block runs. The finally block prints '(done looking)' every single time.

blocks.heading: raise: make your own error 🚨
blocks.html: Sometimes you want to stop and say “that's not allowed.” The raise keyword lets you create your own error with a clear message — and you can even catch it yourself.

blocks.caption: Raising and catching your own error
blocks.explain: raise ValueError(...) makes an error on purpose with a helpful message. We catch it with 'except ValueError as oops', and 'oops' holds the message so we can print it. No crash!

blocks.question: When you read a traceback, which line tells you the kind of error?
blocks.explain: Read tracebacks from the bottom up — the last line names the error type and gives a short hint.

blocks.question: Which error happens when you ask a dictionary for a key it doesn't have?
blocks.explain: A missing dictionary key raises a KeyError. A missing list spot raises an IndexError.

blocks.question: What does the finally block do?
options: Runs only when there is an error
Runs only when there is NO error
Runs no matter what — error or not
blocks.explain: finally always runs, whether or not an error happened. It's perfect for cleanup.

blocks.title: Catch the divide-by-zero
blocks.instructions: Wrap the risky division in a try/except so the program prints a friendly message instead of crashing. Catch ZeroDivisionError and print something kind. Then press Check.
blocks.starter:
a = 8
b = 0
# wrap this in try/except so it doesn't crash:
print(a / b)
blocks.passMsg: Nice — your program now stays calm instead of crashing. 🛟
blocks.failMsg: Make sure you use try:, except ZeroDivisionError:, and keep the division inside the try block.

blocks.title: Safe number reader
blocks.instructions: The variable user_text holds something a user typed. Use try/except to turn it into a number with int(), but catch ValueError and print a friendly message if it isn't a number. Use try, except, and ValueError.
blocks.starter:
user_text = "banana"  # pretend the user typed this
# turn it into a number safely:
blocks.passMsg: Perfect — bad input can't crash you anymore. 💪
blocks.failMsg: Use int(user_text) inside a try block and catch ValueError in the except block.

challenge.title: Build a crash-proof calculator
challenge.instructions: Write a function called safe_calc(a, b) that returns a / b. Use try/except so it NEVER crashes: catch ZeroDivisionError and return a friendly message like "Can't divide by zero!" when b is 0. Then call it twice — once with normal numbers and once dividing by zero — and print both results.
challenge.starter:
def safe_calc(a, b):
    # use try/except here
    pass

# call it twice and print the results
challenge.hint: Put 'return a / b' inside a try block. In the except ZeroDivisionError block, return your friendly message. Then print safe_calc(20, 4) and safe_calc(20, 0).
challenge.passMsg: 🏆 You built a calculator that refuses to crash! That's exactly how real software handles surprises. Day 15 complete!
